html.question: What is the difference between HTML elements and HTML tags?
html.answer:
A tag is the markup written in angle brackets, e.g.

and

. An element is the complete unit — opening tag + content + closing tag. Self-closing tags like and
form a complete element on their own.

html.question: What is the difference between HTML and HTML5?
html.answer: HTML5 (2014) added semantic elements (<header>,<footer>,<article>,<nav>,<section>), native <audio>/<video>, <canvas> for 2D drawing, localStorage/sessionStorage, new input types (email, date, range, color), Geolocation API, and WebSockets. It also deprecated presentational tags like <font> and <center>.

html.question: What is the purpose of the DOCTYPE declaration?
html.answer: <!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser to render in standards mode (HTML5). Without it, browsers enter "quirks mode" and apply legacy rendering rules that differ per browser, breaking modern layouts.

html.question: What is the difference between block-level and inline elements?
html.answer:
Block elements start on a new line and take full parent width:
,

,

–

,
    ,
    . Inline elements flow within text and only take content width: , , , , , . display:inline-block gives block sizing while staying inline.

html.question: What are semantic HTML elements and why do they matter?
html.answer: Semantic elements describe their purpose: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, <footer>. They help screen readers navigate (accessibility/WCAG), help search engines understand page structure (SEO), and make code readable and maintainable.

html.question: How do you create an accessible form with email, password, and submit?
html.answer: Use <label> with matching for/id, appropriate type attributes, and required for validation.
html.code:
<form action="/login" method="POST">
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required />
  <label for="password">Password</label>
  <input type="password" id="password" name="password" required minlength="8" />
  <button type="submit">Sign In</button>
</form>

html.question: What is the alt attribute on and why is it important?
html.answer: alt provides alternative text shown when an image fails to load and read by screen readers. It is essential for: accessibility (WCAG 2.1 requires it), SEO (search engines index it), and UX when images are blocked or slow.

html.question: What is the difference between the GET and POST methods in an HTML form?
html.answer: GET appends data to the URL as query strings — visible in browser history, bookmarkable, max ~2000 chars, never for sensitive data. POST sends data in the request body — not visible in URL, required for passwords, file uploads, and large payloads.

html.question: What is the rendered result of this markup?
html.output: "love" appears bold, "coding" appears italic, all on one line inside a paragraph.
html.explanation: <strong> = bold with semantic importance. <em> = italic with semantic emphasis. Both are inline, so they render within the paragraph flow.

html.question: What is the difference between <section>, <article>, and <div>?
html.answer: <div> is a generic, non-semantic container. <section> groups thematically related content that belongs to a larger page (e.g. chapters). <article> is self-contained, independently distributable content like a blog post, news item, or comment.

html.question: How do you open a link in a new tab securely?
html.answer: Use target="_blank" with rel="noopener noreferrer". Without rel, the new page can access window.opener, enabling tabnapping phishing attacks.

html.question: What does this meta tag do?
html.output: Makes the page responsive. Sets the viewport width to the device screen width at 1:1 zoom, preventing mobile browsers from rendering at desktop width and scaling down.
html.explanation: Without this, mobile sites appear zoomed-out. Required for responsive CSS media queries to work correctly.

html.question: What are ARIA roles and when should you use them?
html.answer: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) adds accessibility semantics to elements. Use it only when native HTML semantics are insufficient. Rule 1 of ARIA: use native HTML first. Examples: role="dialog" on custom modals, aria-label on icon-only buttons, aria-live for dynamic updates.

html.question: What is the difference between async and defer on a <script> tag?
html.answer: Both download scripts in parallel with HTML parsing. async executes immediately when downloaded, interrupting parsing — suited for independent scripts (analytics). defer executes after HTML parsing is complete in document order — suited for DOM-manipulating scripts. Neither blocks parsing during download.

html.question: What is the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies?
html.answer: localStorage: ~5MB, persists until explicitly cleared, not sent with requests. sessionStorage: ~5MB, cleared when tab closes, tab-isolated. Cookies: ~4KB, sent with every HTTP request (overhead), can be httpOnly (no JS access), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite (CSRF protection), set expiry date.

html.question: How do you use data-* attributes and access them in JavaScript?
html.answer: data-* attributes store custom data on elements. Access via dataset.camelCase in JS.
html.code:
<button data-product-id="42" data-action="add-to-cart">Add</button>

const btn = document.querySelector("button");
console.log(btn.dataset.productId); // "42"
console.log(btn.dataset.action);    // "add-to-cart"

html.question: What is the difference between <script type="module"> and a regular script?
html.answer: Module scripts: have their own scope (no global pollution), support import/export, always deferred, strict mode by default, loaded once and cached, CORS-restricted. Regular scripts run in global scope, load synchronously unless async/defer.

html.question: What are Web Workers and when would you use them?
html.answer: Web Workers run JavaScript on a background thread separate from the UI thread, communicating via postMessage(). Use for CPU-intensive tasks (image processing, complex calculations, parsing large datasets) that would otherwise freeze the UI. Workers cannot access the DOM.

html.question: What is the difference in behaviour between these two inputs?
html.output: type="search" adds a clear (×) button, triggers the search event on Enter, and carries accessibility semantics for search. Visually similar but semantically and functionally different.
html.explanation: Use type="search" for search fields — screen readers announce it differently.

choices.label: search shows clear button and fires search events

html.question: What is the purpose of <template> in HTML?
html.answer: <template> holds inert HTML that is not rendered or executed when the page loads. The content is parsed and held in a DocumentFragment, cloned and inserted into the DOM dynamically with JavaScript. Used for reusable UI patterns without framework overhead.

html.question: How do you implement lazy loading for images in HTML?
html.answer: Use the native loading="lazy" attribute on . The browser defers loading until the image enters the viewport.

html.question: What is the difference between <link rel="preload"> and <link rel="prefetch">?
html.answer: preload: fetches the resource immediately at high priority for the CURRENT page (fonts, critical CSS, hero images). prefetch: fetches at low priority for the NEXT expected navigation. preload has a must-use-within-a-few-seconds warning; prefetch is speculative.

html.question: What is the difference between <meter> and <progress> elements?
html.answer: <progress> represents completion of a task (no defined range necessarily). <meter> represents a scalar measurement within a defined range (e.g. disk usage). <meter> supports min, max, low, high, optimum attributes for color-coded feedback.

html.question: How do you make a table accessible?
html.answer: Use scope on <th>, caption element, and summary/aria-label on complex tables.

html.question: What is the Critical Rendering Path and how does HTML structure affect it?
html.answer: CRP: Parse HTML → Build DOM → Parse CSS → Build CSSOM → Merge into Render Tree → Layout → Paint → Composite. Optimisations: inline critical CSS above-the-fold, defer/async non-critical JS, preload key fonts, use link rel="preconnect" for external origins, lazy-load below-fold images.

html.question: What are Web Components and what are their three main specifications?
html.answer: 1. Custom Elements — define new HTML tags (class MyEl extends HTMLElement). 2. Shadow DOM — encapsulated DOM + CSS scoping within a components shadow root. 3. HTML Templates — <template> and <slot> for reusable markup that is not rendered until cloned. Together they enable framework-agnostic reusable components.

html.question: How does the browser parse HTML when it encounters a render-blocking resource?
html.answer: CSS is render-blocking — browser must fetch and build CSSOM before painting. Scripts without defer/async are parser-blocking — stop HTML parsing until downloaded and executed. Font loading blocks text rendering (FOIT/FOUT). Solutions: inline critical CSS, defer JS, font-display:swap, preload key resources.

html.question: What is the Content Security Policy (CSP) and how is it configured?
html.answer: CSP is an HTTP response header or <meta> that restricts which sources can load scripts, styles, images etc. Prevents XSS by blocking inline scripts and unknown origins. Example: Content-Security-Policy: default-src self; script-src self cdn.example.com; style-src self unsafe-inline

html.question: What is the difference between Core Web Vitals — LCP, FID/INP, and CLS?
html.answer: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): loading — time for largest visible element. INP (Interaction to Next Paint, replaced FID): responsiveness — max delay from input to visual response. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): visual stability — unexpected layout movement. Google uses these for Search ranking.

html.question: What does this <link> tag do and when should you use it?
html.output: Pre-establishes a connection (DNS + TCP + TLS handshake) to fonts.googleapis.com before any resource is requested. Saves 100–500ms on first font request.
html.explanation: Use preconnect for origins you know will be needed. crossorigin is needed for CORS font requests.

html.question: What are resource hints and when do you use each?
html.answer: preload: must-have current-page resource (critical CSS, hero image, fonts). prefetch: next-page resource at low priority. preconnect: pre-establish TCP+TLS to known origin. dns-prefetch: pre-resolve DNS only — lighter, use for many third-party origins. modulepreload: preload ES module graph.

html.question: How does the Shadow DOM provide style encapsulation?
html.answer: Shadow DOM attaches an isolated DOM subtree to a host element. Styles inside the shadow root do not leak out; external styles do not leak in (except inherited properties and CSS custom properties). Web Components use this for reuse without style collisions. ::slotted() and :host() selectors cross the boundary.

html.question: How do you implement intersection observer for infinite scroll?
html.answer: IntersectionObserver fires when a sentinel element enters the viewport.

html.question: What is the difference between a progressive web app (PWA) and a regular web app?
html.answer: PWAs use: Service Worker (background caching/offline), Web App Manifest (installable, splash screen, theme color), HTTPS (required). Features: offline-first, push notifications, background sync. Lighthouse audits PWA compliance. Not a separate build — same web tech with progressive enhancement.

html.question: What is the purpose of the element versus ?
html.answer: srcset provides resolution/size hints — browser CHOOSES best image. <picture> with <source> provides ART DIRECTION — explicitly switch images at breakpoints or based on media queries. Use srcset for same image at different resolutions; <picture> for entirely different crops per viewport.

html.question: What happens when this form is submitted?
html.output: GET request to the current page URL with ?q=hello appended. Default form action is the current URL; default method is GET.
html.explanation: Without action, the form submits to the current page. Without method, GET is used. The query string is appended to the URL.

choices.label: GET request to current URL with ?q=hello

css.question: What is CSS specificity and how is it calculated?
css.answer: Specificity determines which rule wins: Inline styles=1000, ID selectors=100, Class/attribute/pseudo-classes=10, Element/pseudo-elements=1. Sum the scores — highest wins. !important beats all. Equal specificity = last rule wins (cascade).

css.question: Explain the CSS Box Model. What is the difference between content-box and border-box?
css.answer: Every element = content + padding + border + margin. content-box (default): declared width applies to content only. Total = width + padding + border. border-box: width includes padding and border. Total = width. Use box-sizing:border-box globally (*, *::before, *::after) for predictable sizing.

css.question: What is the total rendered width of this element?
css.explanation: content-box adds padding and border OUTSIDE the declared width. With border-box it would be exactly 200px.

css.question: What is the difference between display:none, visibility:hidden, and opacity:0?
css.answer: display:none: removed from layout entirely — no space, inaccessible to screen readers. visibility:hidden: hidden but space preserved — inaccessible. opacity:0: invisible but space preserved AND still responds to pointer events, still accessible. Use carefully.

css.question: What is the difference between position: static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky?
css.answer: static: default, document flow. relative: offset from own position, stays in flow. absolute: removed from flow, positioned relative to nearest positioned ancestor. fixed: relative to viewport, stays on scroll. sticky: in flow until scroll threshold, then fixed within scroll container.

css.question: How do you centre a div both horizontally and vertically (3 methods)?
css.answer: Flexbox, Grid, and absolute+transform are the three modern approaches.

css.question: What is the computed font-size of .child?
css.explanation: em is relative to the PARENT computed font-size, not the root. Use rem (root em) to avoid compounding.

css.question: How do you create and use a CSS custom property (variable)?
css.answer: Declare with -- prefix on :root for global scope. Access with var(). Can have a fallback value.

css.question: What is the difference between margin and padding?
css.answer: Padding is space INSIDE the border (between content and border) — takes the element background. Margin is space OUTSIDE the border (between elements). Margins collapse vertically — two adjacent vertical margins merge into the larger one. Padding does not collapse.

css.question: What are pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements? Give examples.
css.answer: Pseudo-classes style elements in a specific STATE: :hover, :focus, :nth-child(), :not(), :checked (single colon). Pseudo-elements style a specific PART of an element: ::before, ::after, ::first-line, ::placeholder, ::selection (double colon recommended).

css.question: How do you create a CSS transition?
css.answer: transition shorthand: property duration timing-function delay.

css.question: Which color wins — and why?
css.output: red — the ID selector (#box, specificity=100) beats the class (.text, specificity=10).
css.explanation: Regardless of order in the stylesheet, specificity hierarchy determines the winner. IDs beat classes.

css.question: What is CSS Grid and how does it differ from Flexbox?
css.answer: Flexbox is one-dimensional — align items in a row OR column. CSS Grid is two-dimensional — control rows AND columns simultaneously. Use Flexbox for component internals (nav items, button groups). Use Grid for page-level layout (header + sidebar + main + footer). They complement each other.

css.question: How do you create a 3-column equal grid with a gap?
What is the difference between em, rem, vw, vh, and %?
css.answer: em: relative to parent font-size (compounds). rem: relative to root (html) font-size — predictable. vw/vh: percentage of viewport width/height — good for full-screen layouts. %: relative to parent dimension. Use rem for typography, vw/vh for full-layout sections.

css.question: How do you implement a dark mode using CSS custom properties and prefers-color-scheme?
What are the column widths for this grid at 600px total width?
css.explanation: fr units divide available space proportionally after fixed values and gaps are accounted for.

css.question: What is the stacking context and what creates a new one?
css.answer: A stacking context is an independent layer in the z rendering order. Elements within a context stack relative to each other, not the global z-axis. Created by: position + z-index != auto, opacity < 1, transform, filter, will-change, isolation:isolate, mix-blend-mode. z-index only works within the same stacking context.

css.question: How do you create a responsive card grid that auto-fills based on minimum item width?
css.answer: Use auto-fill with minmax for a truly responsive grid with no media queries.

css.question: What is the difference between @keyframes animation and CSS transition?
css.answer: Transitions: triggered by state change (hover/focus/class toggle), two states only, simpler. Animations (@keyframes): run automatically or on trigger, multiple keyframe steps, loop control, delay without needing state change, pauseable with animation-play-state.

css.question: What is will-change and when should you use it?
css.answer: will-change hints to the browser that an element will be animated, prompting GPU layer promotion in advance. Use sparingly: only for elements you are about to animate, applied just before the animation starts, removed after. Overuse consumes GPU memory and hurts performance.

css.question: How do you truncate overflowing text with an ellipsis?
css.answer: Single-line and multi-line truncation techniques.

css.question: What is BEM naming methodology?
css.answer: BEM (Block, Element, Modifier): Block = standalone component (.card). Element = part of block (.card__title, .card__image). Modifier = variant (.card--featured, .button--large). Avoids specificity wars by keeping all selectors at class level (specificity=10). Pairs well with CSS Modules.

css.question: Is this animation GPU-accelerated?
css.output: NOT GPU-accelerated — animating left causes layout reflow on every frame. Use transform: translateX(200px) instead.
css.explanation: Only transform and opacity are composited on the GPU by default. left/top/margin trigger layout.

choices.label: Not GPU-accelerated — left causes layout reflow

css.question: What are CSS Cascade Layers (@layer)?
css.answer: @layer creates explicit specificity tiers. Rules in lower layers always lose to higher layers regardless of selector specificity, eliminating !important battles in design systems. Syntax: @layer reset, base, components, utilities. Unlayered styles beat all layered styles.

css.question: What is CSS containment and what are the four types?
css.answer: contain property isolates element rendering: size (element does not depend on children for sizing), layout (descendants do not affect outside layout), style (counter scoping), paint (descendants do not paint outside the element). content=layout paint is the most common combo. Improves rendering performance of large pages.

css.question: How do you build a CSS-only sticky header with a frosted glass effect?
css.answer: Sticky positioning + backdrop-filter for glassmorphism.

css.question: What is CSS Houdini and what problem does it solve?
css.answer: Houdini exposes browser rendering engine hooks to JavaScript. APIs: Paint Worklet (custom CSS images), Layout Worklet (custom layout algorithms), Animation Worklet (compositor-threaded animations), Typed OM (type-safe CSS in JS). Solves the problem of polyfilling CSS features that previously required DOM manipulation and JS overhead.

css.question: Explain the difference between logical properties and physical properties in CSS.
css.answer: Physical: margin-left, padding-top — depend on screen direction. Logical: margin-inline-start, padding-block-start — depend on writing-mode and direction. Use logical properties for proper RTL support and vertical writing modes. Example: margin-inline (= left+right in LTR) instead of margin-left + margin-right.

css.question: What is the output? Does margin collapsing apply?
css.output: Parent and child both get a 30px top margin (the larger one). The child margin collapses into the parent margin — only one margin appears.
css.explanation: Margin collapsing: adjacent vertical margins on parent+first-child merge when no border/padding separates them. Adding padding-top:1px to parent prevents collapse.

css.question: What are CSS subgrid and how is it different from a nested grid?
css.answer: A nested grid creates its own independent grid tracks. subgrid (grid-template-columns:subgrid) makes a child participate in the PARENT grid tracks, enabling perfect column alignment between nested components. Crucial for design systems where you want card content to align across sibling cards.

css.question: How do you create a smooth scroll-driven animation using just CSS?
css.answer: CSS scroll-driven animations (2023) animate based on scroll position without JavaScript.

css.question: What is the performance difference between CSS Grid and absolute positioning for complex layouts?
css.answer: Absolute positioning removes elements from flow — the browser cannot use them for layout calculations, but calculating each position independently is expensive for many elements. Grid uses layout algorithms that compute positions together efficiently. Grid typically outperforms absolute positioning for grid-like layouts and is more maintainable.

css.question: How do you implement container queries and when are they better than media queries?
css.answer: Container queries style an element based on its CONTAINER size, not the viewport. Use when the same component appears in different column widths. A card inside a 3-col grid vs 1-col should respond to its container, not the page width.

css.question: What is the @property rule in CSS and how does it enable animated custom properties?
css.answer: @property registers a custom property with a type, initial value, and inheritance flag. This lets the browser interpolate between values during animation — otherwise custom property changes are not interpolatable. Enables smooth gradient transitions, numeric animations.

css.question: How do you achieve CSS-only smooth animations that do not block the main thread?
css.answer: Use properties that the compositor can animate without involving layout and paint: transform and opacity. Promote elements that will animate with will-change.

flexbox.question: What is Flexbox in CSS?
flexbox.answer: Flexbox (Flexible Box Layout) is a one-dimensional layout model used to distribute space dynamically and align items within a container (either in a row or a column).

flexbox.question: How do you turn an element into a flex container?
flexbox.answer: By applying the CSS property display: flex; (or inline-flex) to the parent container.

flexbox.question: What defines the main axis and cross axis in Flexbox?
flexbox.answer: The main axis is defined by flex-direction (row is horizontal, column is vertical). The cross axis is perpendicular to the main axis.

flexbox.question: How do you center an item perfectly vertically and horizontally using Flexbox?
What is the default value of flex-direction?
flexbox.answer: row (left to right in LTR languages).

flexbox.question: What property aligns items along the main axis?
flexbox.answer: justify-content. Common values: flex-start, center, flex-end, space-between, space-around, space-evenly.

flexbox.question: What property aligns items along the cross axis?
flexbox.answer: align-items for the current row/column. Common values: stretch, center, flex-start, flex-end, baseline.

flexbox.question: What does flex-wrap do?
flexbox.answer: By default, flex items try to fit onto one line. flex-wrap: wrap; allows items to break onto multiple lines if there is not enough space.

flexbox.question: What happens when an image is placed inside a flex container by default?
flexbox.output: The image will stretch vertically because the default align-items is stretch.

flexbox.question: What is the difference between space-between and space-around?
flexbox.answer: space-between pushes the first item to the start edge and the last to the end edge. space-around gives equal space around every item (half space on ends).

flexbox.question: What does flex-grow do?
flexbox.answer: It defines the ability for a flex item to grow if necessary. It accepts a unitless proportion (e.g., flex-grow: 1).

flexbox.question: Make the second item take up all remaining space
What is the difference between align-items and align-content?
flexbox.answer: align-items aligns items within a single flex line on the cross axis. align-content aligns the lines themselves when there is extra space on the cross axis (requires flex-wrap: wrap).

flexbox.question: How do you change the order of flex items without changing HTML?
What does the flex shorthand property combine?
flexbox.answer: flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis. E.g., flex: 1 0 200px;

flexbox.question: What happens if you set flex: 1?
flexbox.answer: It evaluates to flex: 1 1 0%. The item can stretch and shrink, starting from a base width of 0.

flexbox.question: What is flex-basis?
flexbox.answer: It defines the default size of an element before the remaining space is distributed. It overrides the width (or height if column) property.

flexbox.question: What is the difference between flex-basis: 0 and flex-basis: auto?
flexbox.answer: flex-basis: 0 distributes space ignoring the content size. flex-basis: auto uses the content's intrinsic size before distributing space.

flexbox.question: Create a responsive sticky footer using Flexbox
What does flex-shrink do?
flexbox.answer: It defines the ability for a flex item to shrink if necessary. The default is 1 (it will shrink). Set to 0 to prevent shrinking.

flexbox.question: Prevent an icon from shrinking in a flex row
What is align-self?
flexbox.answer: It allows the default alignment (or the one specified by align-items) to be overridden for individual flex items.

flexbox.question: What happens to floats inside a flex container?
flexbox.output: Floats are automatically ignored. The element behaves as a flex item, and float has no effect.

flexbox.question: Can you use margins with flex items?
flexbox.answer: Yes. A useful trick: setting margin: auto on a flex item absorbs extra space, easily pushing it to the far edge.

flexbox.question: Why would you use Flexbox over CSS Grid?
flexbox.answer: Flexbox is highly optimized for 1D layouts (a single row or column) and flow direction where content dictates size. Grid is intended for strict 2D layouts (rows and columns simultaneously).

flexbox.question: What causes the "flexbug" where text does not truncate properly (text-overflow: ellipsis)?
flexbox.answer: By default, flex items have min-width: auto. This prevents child elements from shrinking below their content size. Fix: apply min-width: 0 on the flex item.

flexbox.question: How does accessibility relate to the order property?
flexbox.answer: The order property changes visual appearance but NOT the logical DOM structure. Screen readers and keyboard navigation (Tab key) follow the DOM order, creating a confusing mismatch.

flexbox.question: Fix text truncation inside a flex item
What is the gap property in Flexbox?
flexbox.answer: Originally only in Grid, gap is now standard in Flexbox. It specifies the spacing strictly between flex items, removing the need for negative margins and nth-child hacks.

flexbox.question: What does flex: initial imply?
flexbox.answer: Equivalent to flex: 0 1 auto. Items size according to their width/height, shrink if necessary, but will NOT grow to fill extra space.

flexbox.question: How does line-height affect flex containers?
flexbox.answer: Applying align-items: baseline aligns items according to their typography. Changes to line-height alter the alignment baseline points across different font sizes.

flexbox.question: How is extra space distributed when flex-grow values are 1, 2, and 1?
flexbox.output: Extra space is divided into 4 portions (1+2+1). Item 2 gets 50% of the EXTRA space, while 1 and 3 get 25% each.

flexbox.question: What happens if you combine flex-wrap: wrap-reverse and flex-direction: column-reverse?
flexbox.answer: The layout grows bottom-to-top, and when wrapping occurs, new columns are placed to the left (depending on LTR/RTL) instead of the right.

flexbox.question: Push the last element of a navigation bar to the far right using margin.
What is safe and unsafe alignment in Flexbox?
flexbox.answer: New values like align-items: safe center. Safe ensures that if content overflows, it defaults to flex-start rather than centering and cutting off inaccessible top content.

flexbox.question: How do z-index and flex interact?
flexbox.answer: Because flex items automatically establish a new stacking context if their z-index isn't auto, you can use z-index directly on them without needing position: relative.

javascript.question: What is the difference between var, let, and const?
javascript.answer: var: function-scoped, hoisted + initialized to undefined, can be re-declared. let: block-scoped, hoisted in TDZ (cannot access before declaration), can be reassigned. const: block-scoped, must be initialized, cannot be reassigned (but object/array contents can be mutated).

javascript.question: What is hoisting in JavaScript?
javascript.answer: Hoisting moves declarations to the top of their scope before execution. var declarations are hoisted and initialized to undefined. Function declarations are fully hoisted (callable before declaration). let/const are hoisted but stay in the Temporal Dead Zone — accessing throws ReferenceError.

javascript.question: Guess the output
javascript.output: undefined (var is hoisted to undefined), then ReferenceError: Cannot access "y" before initialization
javascript.explanation: var is hoisted and initialized. let is hoisted but in TDZ — any access before the declaration line throws.

javascript.question: What is the difference between == and ===?
javascript.answer: == (loose equality): type coercion before comparison — "5"==5 is true, null==undefined is true. === (strict equality): same type AND same value — "5"===5 is false. Always use === in production code. The only valid use of == is null==undefined (catches both).

javascript.question: Guess the output
javascript.explanation: null==undefined special case. 0 and false coerce via ==. === requires same type.

javascript.question: What is the difference between null and undefined?
javascript.answer: undefined means a variable was declared but no value assigned (default state). null is an explicit "no value" assignment — intentional absence. typeof undefined === "undefined". typeof null === "object" (historical bug). null == undefined is true; null === undefined is false.

javascript.question: How do you remove duplicates from an array?
javascript.answer: Set stores only unique values. Spread or Array.from converts back to array.

javascript.question: What is the difference between map(), filter(), and reduce()?
javascript.answer: map: transforms each element, returns same-length array. filter: keeps elements where callback returns truthy, returns subset. reduce: accumulates all elements into a single value (sum, object, flat array). All are non-mutating and return new values.

javascript.question: Guess the output
javascript.output: 5 — arrays are reference types. arr2 = arr copies the REFERENCE, not the array. Both point to the same object.
javascript.explanation: To copy an array: [...arr], arr.slice(), or Array.from(arr). For deep copy: structuredClone().

javascript.question: What is a Promise and what are its three states?
javascript.answer: A Promise represents a future value from an async operation. States: pending (initial, not settled), fulfilled (operation succeeded, .then fires), rejected (operation failed, .catch fires). Once settled (fulfilled or rejected), state is immutable. Use async/await as syntactic sugar.

javascript.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous JavaScript?
javascript.answer: Synchronous: each line waits for the previous to complete — simple but blocks UI. Asynchronous: operations run in background (network, timers, file I/O), callback/Promise/await notifies completion. JavaScript is single-threaded — async prevents blocking the event loop.

javascript.question: What is the output?
javascript.output: 99 — objects are reference types. "copy = obj" copies the reference. Mutating copy.a mutates the original.
javascript.explanation: Shallow copy: { ...obj } or Object.assign(). Deep copy: structuredClone(obj).

javascript.question: What is a closure in JavaScript?
javascript.answer: A closure is a function that retains access to its outer scope variables even after the outer function has returned. The inner function carries a reference to the outer scope (not a copy). Uses: data encapsulation/private variables, function factories, memoization, event handlers with persistent config.

javascript.question: Implement a debounce function
javascript.answer: Debounce delays execution until activity pauses. Clears timer on each call.

javascript.question: Guess the console output order
javascript.explanation: Sync runs first (C). Microtask queue drains completely before macrotasks: Promise.then (B), queueMicrotask (D). Then macrotask: setTimeout (A).

javascript.question: What are call(), apply(), and bind()?
javascript.answer: All set the this context. call(thisArg, arg1, arg2): invokes immediately, args listed. apply(thisArg, [args]): invokes immediately, args as array. bind(thisArg, ...partialArgs): returns a new permanently-bound function, does not invoke. bind is used to create partially-applied or event handler functions.

javascript.question: Implement a throttle function
javascript.answer: Throttle allows only one call per interval. Uses a timer flag.

javascript.question: What is event bubbling and event capturing?
javascript.answer: Event bubbling (default): event fires on target then bubbles UP to ancestors. Capturing: event fires from document DOWN to target (useCapture=true in addEventListener). stopPropagation() stops bubbling/capturing. Event delegation: attach one listener on a parent to handle events from many children via e.target.

javascript.question: Guess the output
javascript.output: 3, 3, 3 — var is function-scoped, all three closures share the same i which is 3 after the loop.
javascript.explanation: Fix with let (block-scoped per iteration) or setTimeout(()=>console.log(i), 0, i) passing i as arg.

javascript.question: What are WeakMap and WeakSet and why would you use them?
javascript.answer: WeakMap: keys must be objects, held weakly — key can be garbage-collected when no other references exist. WeakSet: stores objects weakly. Neither is iterable (no .forEach, no size). Use: attaching metadata to DOM elements without memory leaks, implementing private class fields, memoization with DOM nodes.

javascript.question: How do you implement memoization?
javascript.answer: Cache function results keyed by arguments.

javascript.question: What is the Prototype chain?
javascript.answer: Every JavaScript object has an internal [[Prototype]] link. When a property is not found, JS traverses the chain until null is reached. Arrays inherit from Array.prototype, which inherits from Object.prototype, then null. Object.create(proto) creates an object with a specific prototype. class syntax is syntactic sugar over prototype chains.

javascript.question: What does this output?
javascript.explanation: name is own property (set by constructor). greet is on the prototype — hasOwnProperty returns false for prototype properties.

javascript.question: How do you use Promise.all vs Promise.allSettled vs Promise.race?
Explain the JavaScript event loop in detail — call stack, task queue, microtask queue.
javascript.answer: Call Stack: executes sync code LIFO. Web APIs: browser handles async (setTimeout, fetch, DOM events). Task Queue (macrotasks): setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks — one task per loop iteration. Microtask Queue: Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver — entire queue drains after each task before next task. process.nextTick in Node runs before microtasks.

javascript.question: What is CommonJS vs ES Modules? What are the key differences?
javascript.answer: CJS (require/module.exports): synchronous, runtime evaluation, dynamic (can require conditionally), outputs a copy. ESM (import/export): static (analyzed at parse time = tree-shaking), async loading, live bindings (not copies), top-level await, works natively in browsers. Node.js supports both; .mjs or "type":"module" in package.json for ESM.

javascript.question: Implement a deep clone function without using JSON.parse/JSON.stringify
javascript.answer: JSON loses Dates, undefined, functions, circular refs. Use structuredClone() in modern environments.

javascript.question: What are Generators and how do they work?
javascript.answer: Generators are functions that can pause (yield) and resume, maintaining internal state. function* syntax. Returns an iterator. Each yield pauses; .next() resumes. Use cases: lazy infinite sequences, async flow control (before async/await), implementing custom iterators/iterables, pagination logic.

javascript.question: What does this output and why?
javascript.output: "Hello undefined" — arrow functions do NOT have their own this. They inherit this from the enclosing LEXICAL scope, which is the module/global scope where name is undefined.
javascript.explanation: Use regular function for methods that need their own this. Arrow functions are correct for callbacks inside methods.

javascript.question: What are Proxy and Reflect in JavaScript?
javascript.answer: Proxy wraps an object and intercepts operations (get, set, has, delete, apply, construct) via handler traps. Reflect provides the default behavior for each trap. Use: validation, logging, reactive data (Vue 3 uses Proxy for reactivity), API mocking, deep read-only objects.

javascript.question: What is memory management in JavaScript and what causes memory leaks?
javascript.answer: JS uses automatic garbage collection (mark-and-sweep). Memory leaks: (1) accidentally global variables (missing let/const), (2) forgotten timers/intervals still referencing DOM nodes, (3) closures holding large objects in scope, (4) DOM references after node removal, (5) growing caches without eviction. Use Chrome DevTools Memory tab to detect.

javascript.question: Implement a simple Observable (pub/sub) pattern
What are tagged template literals and what are they used for?
javascript.answer: Tagged templates call a function with the string parts and interpolated values as arguments. The tag controls rendering/escaping. Used for: SQL query sanitization (sql`SELECT * FROM ${table}`), styled-components CSS, i18n, HTML sanitization, GraphQL queries (gql`...`).

javascript.question: What is the difference between Object.freeze(), Object.seal(), and Object.preventExtensions()?
javascript.answer: preventExtensions: cannot add new properties, can modify/delete existing. seal: cannot add or delete, can modify values. freeze: cannot add, delete, or modify — shallow immutability. None deep-freeze nested objects. Use Object.freeze recursively or structuredClone+freeze for deep immutability.

javascript.question: Write a function that flattens a deeply nested array
What does Symbol ensure here?
javascript.output: ["name"] — Symbol keys are excluded from Object.keys(). JSON.stringify also ignores Symbol keys.
javascript.explanation: Symbols are hidden from enumeration. Access with Object.getOwnPropertySymbols(). Use for private/non-conflicting keys in objects.

react.question: What is the Virtual DOM and how does React use it?
react.answer: The Virtual DOM is an in-memory JS representation of the real DOM. On state change: React re-renders the virtual tree, diffs it against the previous tree (reconciliation), and applies only the minimum real DOM mutations (patching). This batches DOM writes and avoids expensive full re-renders.

react.question: What is the difference between a functional component and class component?
react.answer: Functional components: plain JS functions returning JSX, use Hooks for state/effects, simpler to write and test. Class components: extend React.Component, use this.state and lifecycle methods (componentDidMount etc). Hooks replaced nearly all class component use cases. New code should always use functional components.

react.question: What is the difference between props and state?
react.answer: Props: read-only data passed from parent to child. The child cannot modify props. State: mutable data owned by the component. Calling useState setter triggers a re-render. When state or props change, React re-renders the component and descendants (unless memoized).

react.question: Guess when this useEffect fires
react.output: On initial mount AND every time count changes.
react.explanation: Empty [] = only on mount. [count] = mount + every update where count changed. No array = every render.

choices.label: On mount and when count changes

react.question: How do you correctly update state that depends on the previous value?
react.answer: Use the updater form to avoid stale closure bugs, especially in async contexts or batched updates.

react.question: What are React keys and why are they important in lists?
react.answer: Keys are unique identifiers on list items that help React identify which items changed, were added, or removed during reconciliation. Without keys (or with index keys on reordered lists), React cannot efficiently diff lists and may incorrectly reuse components, causing subtle state bugs.

react.question: How many times does this component re-render when the button is clicked once?
react.output: Once — React 18 automatic batching merges both setState calls into a single re-render.
react.explanation: React 18 batches all state updates in event handlers, async functions, and more. In React 17 this was ONE render in handlers but TWO in async code.

react.question: What is JSX and how does it compile?
react.answer: JSX is a syntax extension that looks like HTML in JS. Babel compiles JSX to React.createElement() calls. Since React 17+, the new JSX transform auto-imports jsx from react/jsx-runtime, so you no longer need import React from "react" in every file.

react.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components?
react.answer: Controlled: React state is the single source of truth. Every keystroke updates state via onChange. Uncontrolled: DOM manages its own state. Access value via ref. Use controlled for validation, conditional logic, resetting. Use uncontrolled for simple file inputs or when integrating non-React libraries.

react.question: What does useEffect return and when should you use it?
react.answer: useEffect can optionally return a cleanup function that runs before the next effect or when the component unmounts. Use cleanup to: clear timers, cancel subscriptions, abort fetch requests, remove event listeners — prevents memory leaks.

react.question: What is React.Fragment and why is it used?
react.answer: Fragment (<React.Fragment> or shorthand <>) groups multiple elements without adding an extra DOM node. Use when a component must return siblings without a wrapping div that would break CSS layouts (e.g. flex/grid children, table rows). <Fragment key={id}> is needed when mapping with keys.

react.question: How do you conditionally render elements in React?
react.answer: Three common patterns: ternary, logical &&, and early return.

react.question: What is prop drilling and how does Context API solve it?
react.answer: Prop drilling: passing data through many intermediate components that do not need it, just to reach a deep child. Context API: createContext + Provider wraps a subtree; any descendant calls useContext(MyContext) to consume data directly, skipping intermediaries. Avoid Context for high-frequency state — use Zustand/Redux for that.

react.question: When should you use useCallback and useMemo?
react.answer: useMemo: memoizes a computed VALUE — prevents expensive recalculation on every render. useCallback: memoizes a FUNCTION REFERENCE — prevents child re-renders when a callback prop changes. Only apply after profiling shows a real perf issue — both add overhead per render and cognitive complexity.

react.question: What is the value of count after handleClick runs?
react.output: 1 — all three calls use the same stale count=0 from the render closure. Each sets it to 0+1=1.
react.explanation: Fix with functional updater: setCount(c => c+1). Three functional updaters would correctly produce 3.

react.question: What is useRef and how is it different from useState?
react.answer: useRef returns a mutable {current} object that persists across renders without triggering a re-render. Use cases: accessing DOM elements (ref.current.focus()), storing mutable values (timers, previous values, animation handles). useState triggers re-renders; useRef does not.

react.question: How do you implement error boundaries in React?
react.answer: Error boundaries catch JS errors in their child tree. They must be class components (no Hook equivalent for error catching yet, though use-error-boundary library wraps this).

react.question: What is React.memo and when does it prevent re-renders?
react.answer: React.memo wraps a functional component and does a shallow prop comparison. If props are shallowly equal, the component skips re-rendering. Does NOT help when: parent passes new object/array/function literals (new reference each render) — combine with useMemo/useCallback. Does not memoize on context changes.

react.question: How do you implement a custom hook for data fetching?
What is the useReducer hook and when should you prefer it over useState?
react.answer: useReducer(reducer, initialState) manages complex state with a (state, action) => newState pure function, similar to Redux. Prefer over useState when: multiple related state values, next state depends on previous in complex ways, deeply nested updates, or you want the dispatch/action pattern for predictable state transitions.

react.question: What is React Suspense and what is it used for?
react.answer: Suspense shows a fallback UI while child components are "suspended" (loading). Currently used for: lazy-loaded components (React.lazy + dynamic import), and data fetching in frameworks with Suspense-aware data sources (Relay, Next.js, React Query experimental). Error boundaries catch suspension errors.

react.question: How do you implement a lazy-loaded route with Suspense?
Does this component re-render when parent re-renders?
react.output: YES — Child re-renders every time Parent renders because the arrow function creates a new reference each render, defeating React.memo.
react.explanation: Fix: wrap onClick with useCallback(() => setCount(c => c+1), []) so the reference is stable.

choices.label: Yes — new function reference on each render

react.question: What is the difference between client-side and server-side rendering?
react.answer: CSR: browser downloads empty HTML + JS bundle, renders UI in browser. Slow first load, great interactivity. SSR: server renders full HTML for each request, fast first paint, good SEO. SSG: HTML pre-generated at build time (fastest, great for static content). ISR (Next.js): SSG + periodic regeneration. React 18 RSC blurs lines with streaming.

react.question: What is concurrent rendering in React 18 and what new capabilities does it enable?
react.answer: Concurrent mode lets React pause, interrupt, and resume renders. Features: automatic batching (all updates batched, not just event handlers), useTransition (marks state updates as non-urgent — UI stays responsive), useDeferredValue (defer expensive computation), Suspense improvements, streaming SSR with selectiveHydration, createRoot required.

react.question: What are React Server Components and how do they differ from Client Components?
react.answer: Server Components (RSC) run exclusively on the server: access databases/files directly, no client JS sent, no useState/useEffect, no event handlers. Client Components (use client) run on server + client for hydration. RSC reduce bundle size dramatically. Used in Next.js 13+ App Router. RSC pass data to Client Components as props (serialized).

react.question: How do you prevent stale closures in useEffect?
react.answer: Stale closures capture variables from an older render. Solutions: include dependencies correctly, or use refs.

react.question: What is reconciliation and how does React's diffing algorithm work?
react.answer: Reconciliation is the process of determining minimal DOM mutations after a re-render. React's O(n) heuristic algorithm: 1) Elements of different types get full tree replacement. 2) Same type elements update props. 3) Keys on list items allow efficient reorder vs destroy+recreate. Fiber (React 16+) made reconciliation interruptible for concurrent mode.

react.question: How would you architect state management for a large React application?
react.answer: Local state (useState): component-private UI state. Lifted state: shared between siblings via common parent. Context: cross-cutting concerns — auth, theme, locale (not high-frequency). Zustand/Jotai: lightweight global state with minimal boilerplate. Redux Toolkit: complex state with time-travel debugging, middleware. React Query/SWR: server state, caching, revalidation — do NOT put this in Redux.

react.question: What does this output and is there a problem?
react.output: Renders nothing first (data=null), then re-renders with data.name. Problem: no cleanup → if component unmounts before fetch completes, setData is called on an unmounted component (memory leak / warning in React 17).
react.explanation: Fix: AbortController in cleanup, or use React Query which handles this automatically.

choices.label: Renders correct data but has unmount memory leak

react.question: How does React's new Suspense with streaming SSR work?
react.answer: With React 18 + streaming SSR (renderToPipeableStream): server streams HTML progressively — content above Suspense boundaries is sent immediately. When async data resolves, server sends the remaining HTML inline scripts. The browser can start rendering and hydrating sections independently (selective hydration). Reduces TTFB and TTI for data-heavy pages.

react.question: How do you implement virtualized list rendering for 10,000 items?
react.answer: Virtualization only renders visible items using windowing. react-window is the standard library.

react.question: What testing strategies do you use for React components?
react.answer: Unit: test individual components in isolation with React Testing Library (query by role/text, not class). Integration: test user flows across multiple components. RTL philosophy: test behavior not implementation (avoid testing internal state). Mock: msw (Mock Service Worker) for API calls. E2E: Playwright or Cypress for critical paths. Jest + RTL is the standard stack.

react.question: What is the difference between useLayoutEffect and useEffect?
react.answer: useEffect: fires asynchronously after the browser paints — no blocking. useLayoutEffect: fires synchronously after DOM mutations but BEFORE the browser paints. Use useLayoutEffect only for: reading DOM dimensions/positions after render to avoid visual flicker (e.g., tooltip positioning, scroll restoration). Warning: not available in SSR.

react.question: Implement a compound component pattern
react.answer: Compound components share implicit state through Context instead of prop drilling.

react.question: How do you measure and improve React app performance?
react.answer: Measure: React DevTools Profiler (find slow renders, their causes), Chrome Performance tab, Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS). Fix slow renders: React.memo, useMemo, useCallback. Fix bundle size: code splitting (lazy+Suspense), analyze with webpack-bundle-analyzer. Fix lists: virtualization (react-window). Fix images: next/image, lazy loading. Fix fonts: font-display:swap, preload.

node-js.question: What is Node.js and why is it used for backend development?
node-js.answer: Node.js is a runtime that executes JavaScript outside the browser using the V8 engine. It uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model — one thread handles thousands of concurrent connections by delegating I/O to the OS. Popular for: REST APIs, real-time apps (chat, live updates), CLI tools, microservices.

node-js.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Synchronous: blocks the event loop until complete — nothing else runs. Asynchronous: registers a callback/Promise, delegates work to the OS or thread pool, and returns immediately. Blocking the event loop (readFileSync in a route) is a critical mistake — all requests freeze.

node-js.question: Guess the console output order
node-js.explanation: Microtasks (Promises, queueMicrotask) always drain before the next macrotask (setTimeout/setInterval).

node-js.question: How do you use environment variables safely in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Store in .env (never commit), load with dotenv, validate at startup.

node-js.question: What is package.json and what are the key fields?
node-js.answer: package.json is the project manifest. Key fields: name, version, main (entry point), scripts (npm run commands), dependencies (production), devDependencies (dev-only), engines (Node.js version), type ("module" for ESM), exports (sub-path entry points).

node-js.question: What is the difference between dependencies and devDependencies?
node-js.answer: dependencies: required at runtime in production (express, mongoose, bcrypt). devDependencies: only needed during development/build (nodemon, jest, eslint, TypeScript). npm install --production skips devDependencies. Keeps production containers lean.

node-js.question: How do you read a file asynchronously in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Always prefer async methods in server code to avoid blocking the event loop.

node-js.question: What is npm and what does npm install do?
node-js.answer: npm (Node Package Manager) manages packages. npm install: reads package.json, downloads all dependencies into node_modules, creates/updates package-lock.json (exact versions). npm install <pkg> --save adds to dependencies. --save-dev adds to devDependencies. Lock file ensures reproducible builds.

node-js.question: What does this code do wrong?
node-js.output: readFileSync blocks the entire Node.js event loop on EVERY request. All other requests wait until the file is read. Use fs.promises.readFile() or cache the file at startup.
node-js.explanation: Sync I/O in route handlers is one of the most common and severe Node.js performance mistakes.

choices.label: Blocks event loop on every request — critical bug

node-js.question: What is the difference between require() and import in Node.js?
node-js.answer: require() (CommonJS): synchronous, loads at runtime, works without configuration. import (ESM): static (analyzed before execution), async, tree-shakeable, requires "type":"module" in package.json or .mjs extension. ESM is the modern standard. Mixing is possible but requires care.

node-js.question: What is process.env and how do you access environment variables?
node-js.answer: process.env is an object containing all environment variables from the OS and .env files (via dotenv). Access: process.env.MY_VAR. All values are strings — parse numbers with Number() or parseInt(). Never use process.env.VAR || "default" for secrets; require them explicitly.

node-js.question: How do you create a simple HTTP server in Node.js without Express?
Explain the Node.js event loop phases in order.
node-js.answer: 1. timers — run expired setTimeout/setInterval callbacks. 2. pending callbacks — I/O errors from previous iteration. 3. idle/prepare — internal use. 4. poll — retrieve I/O events, block here if queue empty. 5. check — setImmediate() callbacks. 6. close callbacks — e.g. socket.on("close"). Between EVERY phase: process.nextTick queue drains, then Promise microtask queue drains.

node-js.question: What is the difference between process.nextTick() and setImmediate()?
node-js.answer: process.nextTick: runs BEFORE the next event loop iteration, in the microtask phase (highest priority). setImmediate: runs in the CHECK phase of the current or next iteration (after I/O). nextTick can starve the event loop if called recursively. setImmediate is safer for deferring work after I/O.

node-js.question: What are Node.js streams and what are the four types?
node-js.answer: Streams process data in chunks, avoiding loading everything into memory. Types: Readable (data source: fs.createReadStream), Writable (data sink: res, fs.createWriteStream), Duplex (both: net.Socket), Transform (duplex that transforms: zlib.Gzip). Use stream.pipeline() for error-safe stream composition.

node-js.question: How do you implement rate limiting in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Use express-rate-limit middleware.

node-js.question: What is the worker_threads module and when should you use it?
node-js.answer: worker_threads allows running JavaScript in parallel on separate threads (unlike cluster which forks processes). Use for: CPU-intensive tasks (image processing, crypto, compression). Workers share memory via SharedArrayBuffer and communicate via postMessage. Unlike child_process, workers are lighter and share the same process memory space.

node-js.question: How do you implement graceful shutdown in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Catch SIGTERM/SIGINT, stop accepting new requests, wait for in-flight requests to complete.

node-js.question: What is the module system caching in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Node.js caches modules after the first require(). Subsequent requires() return the same cached exports object — modules are singletons. This means: database connections and configurations are shared across imports. require.cache stores all cached modules. Delete require.cache[require.resolve("./mod")] to force reload.

node-js.question: What is the output?
node-js.output: 1 — Node.js module cache means b.js and c.js get the SAME object reference. Mutation in b.js is visible in c.js.
node-js.explanation: Module exports are cached objects (references). Multiple imports share the same instance.

node-js.question: How do you implement structured logging in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Use a structured logger like Pino (fastest) or Winston.

node-js.question: How does Node.js handle circular dependencies?
node-js.answer: Node.js partially resolves circular requires by returning an incomplete (partial) export object. If module A requires module B which requires module A, B gets whatever A has exported SO FAR. This often causes undefined exports. Solutions: extract shared code to a third module, use lazy requires (require inside a function), or restructure the dependency graph.

node-js.question: How do you validate and sanitize request body in Node.js?
node-js.answer: Use Joi or Zod for schema validation.

node-js.question: What is EventEmitter and how is it used in Node.js?
node-js.answer: EventEmitter is the base class for event-driven programming in Node.js. Most Node.js core modules (http.Server, stream, net) extend it. API: emitter.on(event, listener), emitter.emit(event, ...args), emitter.once(), emitter.off(). Use to decouple components and implement pub/sub within a service.

node-js.question: How does Node.js clustering work and what are its limitations?
node-js.answer: Node.js is single-threaded — uses one CPU. cluster module forks child processes (one per CPU core) sharing the same TCP port via the OS. cluster.isMaster distributes connections. Limitation: no shared memory between workers — use Redis for sessions/caches. In production, use PM2 in cluster mode or container orchestration.

node-js.question: What is backpressure in Node.js streams and how do you handle it?
node-js.answer: Backpressure occurs when a Readable produces data faster than a Writable can consume it, overflowing internal buffers. Readable.pipe() handles it automatically — pauses the readable when writable.write() returns false, resumes on "drain". In manual implementations always check write return and listen for "drain".

node-js.question: How do you prevent common Node.js security vulnerabilities?
node-js.answer: NoSQL injection: sanitize with mongo-sanitize before queries. Command injection: avoid exec() with user input, use execFile(). Path traversal: use path.resolve() and validate within allowedDir. ReDoS: avoid complex regexes on user input; use RE2 library. Prototype pollution: Object.create(null) for user-input objects. Dependency audit: npm audit regularly.

node-js.question: How do you implement a connection pool for database connections?
node-js.answer: Mongoose manages a pool internally. Configure pool size based on expected concurrency.

node-js.question: What is V8 and how do you profile Node.js performance?
node-js.answer: V8 is the C++ JavaScript engine. It uses JIT compilation, hidden classes (shape-based optimization), and inline caching. Profiling tools: --prof flag (V8 CPU profiler), clinic.js (doctor/flamegraph), node --inspect + Chrome DevTools (heap snapshots, CPU profiles), autocannon for load testing, clinic flame for async bottlenecks.

node-js.question: What is the output and why?
node-js.explanation: process.nextTick runs first (before microtasks in Node.js). Then Promise.then (microtask). Then setImmediate (check phase). Note: in browser there is no nextTick.

node-js.question: How do you implement caching in a Node.js API?
node-js.answer: Layer 1 (in-process): node-cache or simple Map for frequently-read, rarely-changed data. Layer 2 (distributed): Redis — shared across instances, survives restarts, supports TTL, pub/sub. Cache-aside pattern: check cache, miss → query DB → write to cache → return. Cache invalidation strategies: TTL, event-based, write-through.

node-js.question: How do you implement a Bull job queue for background processing?
What are the differences between child_process methods: exec, execFile, spawn, fork?
node-js.answer: spawn: streams I/O, no shell, best for large outputs. exec: buffers output, spawns a shell (shell injection risk), good for simple commands. execFile: like exec but no shell — safer for user-provided commands. fork: specialized spawn for Node.js processes only, enables IPC channel for message passing.

node-js.question: How do you implement distributed tracing in a Node.js microservices architecture?
node-js.answer: Use OpenTelemetry SDK: auto-instrument HTTP/DB clients, propagate trace context via W3C TraceContext headers (traceparent), send spans to a collector (Jaeger, Zipkin, Datadog). Each service adds spans with operation name, duration, tags, and links to parent span ID. Critical for debugging latency in distributed systems.

node-js.question: How do you implement a circuit breaker pattern in Node.js?
How do you handle uncaught exceptions and unhandled rejections in production?
node-js.answer: process.on("uncaughtException"): last-resort handler for sync throws. Log and exit — do not continue, process is in unknown state. process.on("unhandledRejection"): catch forgotten .catch(). In Node.js 15+, unhandledRejection causes process exit by default. Best practice: PM2/container restart on crash + structured log before exit + --exit-on-unhandled-rejections flag.

express-js.question: What is Express.js and what does it add over Node's built-in http module?
express-js.answer: Express.js is a minimal Node.js web framework adding: (1) routing with HTTP method handlers and param extraction, (2) middleware pipeline, (3) req/res helpers (res.json(), req.params, req.query, req.body), (4) template engine support, (5) static file serving, (6) global error handling with 4-param middleware.

express-js.question: What is middleware in Express.js?
express-js.answer: Middleware are functions with (req, res, next) signature in the request-response pipeline. Calling next() passes control to the next middleware. Order matters — middleware runs in registration order. Types: application-level (app.use), router-level, error-handling (4 params), built-in (express.json), third-party (cors, helmet).

express-js.question: How do you create a basic REST endpoint in Express?
What happens when a request hits this middleware and no route matches?
express-js.output: Middleware logs the request. Express's internal 404 handler sends "Cannot GET /path".
express-js.explanation: next() passes to the next registered handler. If nothing handles the request, Express's fallback 404 kicks in.

choices.label: Logs the request; default 404 is sent

express-js.question: What are req.params, req.query, and req.body?
express-js.answer: req.params: URL path segments captured by route patterns (/users/:id → req.params.id). req.query: URL query string parsed as object (?page=2&limit=10 → req.query.page). req.body: parsed request body — requires express.json() or express.urlencoded() middleware.

express-js.question: How do you apply middleware only to specific routes?
express-js.answer: Pass middleware as arguments between route path and handler.

express-js.question: What HTTP status codes should your REST API use?
express-js.answer: 200 OK (success GET/PUT), 201 Created (POST success), 204 No Content (DELETE success), 400 Bad Request (validation fail), 401 Unauthorized (not authenticated), 403 Forbidden (authenticated but no permission), 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict (duplicate), 422 Unprocessable Entity (semantic validation), 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error.

express-js.question: How does Express route parameter validation work?
express-js.answer: Use req.params for path variables. Validate with: manual checks, express-validator, or Joi/Zod. app.param() registers a callback that fires whenever a specific parameter appears in any route in that router — useful for loading resources by ID automatically.

express-js.question: What is the output of this code?
express-js.output: First response is sent correctly. Second res.json throws: "Cannot set headers after they are sent to the client" (ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT).
express-js.explanation: Once res.json/res.send/res.end is called, the response is finalized. Always return after sending or use an if-else structure.

express-js.question: What is the purpose of express.Router()?
express-js.answer: Router() creates a mini Express application with its own middleware and route handlers. Mounted at a path in the main app. Benefits: organize routes by feature/version, apply middleware scoped to a group, split into multiple files for maintainability.

express-js.question: What is CORS and how do you enable it in Express?
express-js.answer: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security policy blocking requests from different origins. APIs called from a different domain need CORS headers. Use the cors package in Express. Configure to allow only your frontend origin in production — never use origin:* with credentials.

express-js.question: How do you serve static files in Express?
How do you implement global error handling in Express?
express-js.answer: Define error-handling middleware with FOUR parameters (err, req, res, next). Place it AFTER all routes. Pass errors to it with next(err). Never send multiple responses — always pass to next or respond.

express-js.question: How do you implement authentication middleware in Express?
What is the difference between app.use() and app.get()/app.post()?
express-js.answer: app.use(path, middleware): matches ALL HTTP methods, prefix matching (/api matches /api/v1/users). app.get/post/put/delete/patch: matches ONE specific HTTP method, exact path by default. Use app.use() for middleware, error handlers, and router mounting. Use method-specific handlers for API endpoints.

express-js.question: How do you add request logging middleware with timestamps?
express-js.answer: Use Morgan (quick setup) or custom middleware.

express-js.question: How do you handle file uploads in Express?
express-js.answer: Use multer middleware for multipart/form-data.

express-js.question: What happens in this route?
express-js.output: Sends null or throws CastError (if ID format is invalid). The async error is NOT caught — it becomes an unhandledPromiseRejection in older Node versions. In newer Node/Express 5, async errors are auto-forwarded to error handler.
express-js.explanation: Always wrap async route handlers in try/catch and call next(err), or use express-async-errors/asyncHandler wrapper.

express-js.question: How do you version your Express API?
express-js.answer: Use router-level versioning as a scalable approach.

express-js.question: What is helmet.js and what headers does it set?
express-js.answer: Helmet is a collection of 15 security middlewares. Key headers it sets: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options (clickjacking), X-Content-Type-Options (MIME sniffing), Strict-Transport-Security (force HTTPS), X-XSS-Protection (legacy), Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy. Use as first middleware: app.use(helmet()).

express-js.question: How do you implement pagination in an Express/MongoDB API?
How does express-validator help with request validation?
express-js.answer: express-validator provides a chain API to validate and sanitize request data declaratively.

express-js.question: What is the difference between res.send() and res.json()?
express-js.answer: res.json() sets Content-Type to application/json and JSON.stringify's the argument (handles undefined and circular references by throwing). res.send() auto-detects type: string→text/html, object→JSON, Buffer→binary. For API endpoints always use res.json() — it is explicit and ensures correct Content-Type header.

express-js.question: How do you implement a search endpoint in Express with MongoDB?
What security hardening should every production Express API have?
express-js.answer: 1. helmet() for security headers. 2. cors() with strict origin whitelist. 3. rate limiting (express-rate-limit). 4. express.json({ limit:"10kb" }) to prevent large payload attacks. 5. mongo-sanitize against NoSQL injection. 6. hpp (HTTP Parameter Pollution protection). 7. HTTPS only. 8. Validate/sanitize all input. 9. Audit npm audit regularly.

express-js.question: How do you implement a standardized API response pattern?
How do you implement multitenancy in an Express API?
express-js.answer: Common approaches: 1. Subdomain routing (tenant.app.com) — extract tenant from req.hostname, load tenant config middleware. 2. Path prefix (/api/tenants/:tenantId) — req.params.tenantId. 3. Request header (X-Tenant-ID). Middleware injects tenant context (DB connection, config) onto req. Store tenant-specific data in separate collections or databases.

express-js.question: What is the difference between Express 4 and Express 5?
express-js.answer: Express 5 (released 2024): async route handlers auto-forward errors to error handler (no try/catch needed), path parameters match changed ({id} instead of :id optionally), router.use() works the same, improved Promise support throughout. Migration from Express 4 is mostly adding async error handling.

express-js.question: How do you implement request timeout middleware?
What is the order middleware runs here?
express-js.output: logger → apiRouter middleware chain → notFoundHandler (for routes that don't match) → errorHandler (for errors passed via next(err)). Middleware runs in registration order.
express-js.explanation: Crucial: notFoundHandler must be LAST of regular middleware. errorHandler must be the final middleware with 4 params.

express-js.question: How do you implement API key authentication?
How do you implement request/response compression in Express?
express-js.answer: Use the compression middleware (gzip/brotli). Should be placed near the top, before static and route handlers.

express-js.question: How do you structure a large Express application?
express-js.answer: Layered architecture: (1) routes — only parse params, call controller. (2) controllers — orchestrate, call services, format response. (3) services — business logic, call repositories. (4) repositories/DAL — all DB queries. (5) models — Mongoose schemas. Middleware, config, utils in separate folders. This separation enables unit testing of services without HTTP.

express-js.question: How do you implement idempotency keys to prevent double submissions?
What is the Express Trust Proxy setting and when is it needed?
express-js.answer: When Express is behind a reverse proxy (Nginx, load balancer, Heroku, Render), req.ip and req.secure get the proxy's values, not the client's. Set app.set("trust proxy", 1) to trust the first proxy's X-Forwarded-For header. Required for: rate limiting per real IP, HTTPS detection, geolocation. Always validate proxy headers in production.

express-js.question: How do you implement WebSockets alongside Express?
express-js.answer: Express uses http.Server. Attach Socket.IO or ws to the same server.

mongodb.question: What is MongoDB and how does it differ from relational databases?
mongodb.answer: MongoDB is a NoSQL document database storing BSON (Binary JSON) documents in collections. No fixed schema. No JOINs — embed related data or use $lookup. Horizontal sharding built-in. Relational DBs (MySQL, PostgreSQL) enforce schema and relationships via foreign keys. MongoDB suits flexible, hierarchical, or high-volume data; SQL suits complex relational queries.

mongodb.question: What is a document and a collection in MongoDB?
mongodb.answer: Document: a BSON record (like a JSON object) with key-value pairs. Maximum 16MB. Collection: a set of documents (like a SQL table) — no schema enforcement by default. Documents in the same collection can have different fields. MongoDB uses dynamic schemas which you can optionally validate with JSON Schema.

mongodb.question: What is an ObjectId in MongoDB?
mongodb.answer: ObjectId is a 12-byte BSON type auto-generated as the _id field. Structure: 4-byte timestamp (seconds since epoch), 5-byte random value (machine+process), 3-byte incrementing counter. It is unique across machines. Encodes creation time — ObjectId.getTimestamp() extracts it. Do not use sequential integers as _id in distributed systems.

mongodb.question: What does this query return?
mongodb.output: All documents in the users collection where age is between 25 and 35 (inclusive).
mongodb.explanation: $gte = greater than or equal, $lte = less than or equal. Comparison operators: $gt, $lt, $gte, $lte, $ne, $eq, $in, $nin.

mongodb.question: What happens to this update without an operator?
mongodb.output: Document becomes { _id: ObjectId(...), age: 30 } — the name field is DELETED. Without $set, updateOne replaces the entire document.
mongodb.explanation: ALWAYS use $set for partial updates: db.users.updateOne({ name: "Ali" }, { $set: { age: 30 } }).

mongodb.question: What is the difference between find() and findOne()?
mongodb.answer: findOne(): returns the first matching document (or null) as an object. find(): returns a cursor — you must iterate or call .toArray()/.pretty(). Use findOne() when expecting at most one result (by _id, email). find() for multiple results with optional sorting, limiting, projecting.

mongodb.question: How do you insert multiple documents efficiently?
mongodb.answer: insertMany() is much faster than looping insertOne() because it uses a single network roundtrip.
mongodb.code:
await db.collection('products').insertMany([
  { name: 'Laptop', price: 50000 },
  { name: 'Mouse',  price: 800 },
], { ordered: false }); // ordered:false = continue on error

mongodb.question: What are MongoDB update operators?
mongodb.answer: $set: set fields. $unset: remove fields. $inc: increment/decrement. $push: add to array. $pull: remove from array. $addToSet: push only if not already present. $pop: remove first/last array element. $rename: rename a field. Always use operators in updateOne/updateMany to avoid accidental document replacement.

mongodb.question: What is the difference between deleteOne() and deleteMany()?
mongodb.answer: deleteOne(): deletes the FIRST document matching the filter. deleteMany(): deletes ALL matching documents. deleteMany({}) deletes all documents in a collection (but keeps the collection itself). dropCollection() removes the collection entirely. Always pass a filter to deleteMany in production to avoid mass deletion.

mongodb.question: What does this projection return?
mongodb.output: Only name and email fields for all documents. _id is excluded (must explicitly set _id:0 to hide it, since _id is included by default).
mongodb.explanation: Projection 1 = include, 0 = exclude. Cannot mix (except _id). Use projection to reduce network transfer.

choices.label: All fields except name and email

mongodb.question: What is the MongoDB shell vs MongoDB Compass?
mongodb.answer: MongoDB Shell (mongosh): command-line REPL for running queries, scripts, and administration. MongoDB Compass: GUI for schema visualization, query builder, index management, explain plans, data aggregation. Use Compass for exploration; Shell for scripting and automation. Atlas also has a built-in web-based shell.

mongodb.question: How do you sort, limit, and skip documents?
mongodb.answer: Chaining sort+limit+skip is the standard pagination approach.

mongodb.question: What is the MongoDB aggregation pipeline?
mongodb.answer: The aggregation pipeline processes documents through sequential stages, each transforming the data. Common stages: $match (filter — put early to use indexes), $group (aggregate with accumulators), $project (reshape/add/remove fields), $sort, $limit, $lookup (left outer join), $unwind (deconstruct array), $addFields, $facet (multi-pipeline).

mongodb.question: Write an aggregation to calculate average salary per department
What are MongoDB indexes and what types exist?
mongodb.answer: Indexes speed up queries by building a sorted B-tree structure. Types: Single field, Compound (multiple fields — order matters for sort/range), Multikey (index arrays — auto-detected), Text (full-text search), 2dsphere (geospatial), Unique, Sparse (only index docs where field exists), TTL (auto-delete documents after expiry). Over-indexing hurts write performance.

mongodb.question: What is the ESR Rule for compound indexes?
mongodb.answer: Equality → Sort → Range. Fields used in EQUALITY conditions first, SORT fields next, RANGE conditions last. Example: filter by status="active" (equality), sort by date (sort), filter age > 25 (range) → index: { status:1, date:1, age:1 }. This order allows the index to be used fully for both filtering and sorting.

mongodb.question: What is the $lookup stage in aggregation?
mongodb.answer: $lookup performs a left outer join between two collections. Like SQL LEFT JOIN. Fields from the "joined" collection are added as an array.

mongodb.question: What does explain() tell you?
